context.typical_causes: Chronic multi-constellation GNSS jamming (and, increasingly, spoofing) degrades navigation integrity across the central and eastern Baltic, affecting both aviation and shipping. Reporting describes hotspots persistently co-located with areas of heavy Russian military presence, and by 2025 detected events increasingly combined jamming with spoofed GPS L1 signals.
context.known_actors: Widely attributed by European reporting and analysis to Russian electronic-warfare activity, principally from the Kaliningrad exclave and the St. Petersburg area. On 15 July 2025 the EU Council sanctioned Russia's 841st Separate Electronic Warfare Center over GNSS jamming and spoofing affecting the Baltic states (per Consilium / Militarnyi).
context.interpretation_notes: Treat the Baltic as a CHRONIC adversarial zone: degraded NIC here is the baseline, not the anomaly. Coverage is dense (busy airspace, many ADS-B receivers), so apparent "spikes" can partly reflect observation density rather than a real change in emitter behavior. Distinguish transient overflights near Kaliningrad from sustained wide-area degradation. Attribution to a specific emitter is analytically plausible but rarely court-proof; keep it attributed to reporting.

sources.title: Consilium (EU Council) — Russian hybrid threats: EU lists nine individuals and six entities
sources.note: Official EU listing (15 Jul 2025) of the 841st EW Center over GNSS jamming/spoofing affecting the Baltics.

sources.title: Militarnyi — Europe Announces Sanctions Over GPS Jamming in the Baltics
sources.note: Reachable summary of the 15 Jul 2025 EU sanctions naming the 841st EW Center in Kaliningrad.

sources.title: France 24 — Russia accused of meddling in the GPS systems of Baltic Sea countries
sources.note: Overview of the 2024 Baltic GPS-interference episode and attribution to Russia.

context.typical_causes: Concentrated GNSS jamming and spoofing emanating from the Kaliningrad exclave, degrading navigation integrity for aircraft and vessels in the surrounding airspace and Gulf of Gdansk approaches.
context.known_actors: A 2025 international triangulation study (reported by Defense News) pinpointed two coastal source locations inside Kaliningrad to about one-kilometer precision — near the Okunevo antenna site and the Baltiysk harbor area — both associated with Russian electronic-warfare units. The EU's 15 July 2025 listing of the 841st Separate Electronic Warfare Center names the Kaliningrad region explicitly.
context.interpretation_notes: This is the emitter neighborhood, not just an affected area — the most specific public attribution of any region in this archive. Even so, the published triangulation covers a handful of dated events, not every signal; do not over-generalize "the source is X" to all Baltic degradation. Use this region to anchor emitter-side analysis; use `baltic` for the wider affected footprint.

sources.title: Defense News — Researchers home in on origins of Russia's Baltic GPS jamming
sources.note: Triangulation to Okunevo and Baltiysk (Gdynia Maritime Univ. team), ~1 km precision, spring 2025.

sources.title: Spire Global — GNSS interference report: Russia 2024/2025, Part 1: Kaliningrad & the Baltic Sea
sources.note: Space-based RF/GNSS monitoring characterizing Kaliningrad-area interference.

context.typical_causes: Persistent GNSS jamming and spoofing over the western and northern Black Sea, degrading navigation integrity for shipping (notably the Greater Odesa corridor) and for aircraft over Romanian/Bulgarian airspace. Ukraine's seaports authority warned mariners in July 2024 not to rely on satellite navigation in the area.
context.known_actors: Reporting attributes much of the interference to Russian electronic-warfare activity; analysts describe it partly as a counter-drone measure. Ukrainian naval intelligence stated Russian forces used a former offshore gas platform for GPS spoofing, which Ukrainian forces said they struck in August 2024 (per gCaptain / Baird Maritime / RFE-RL).
context.interpretation_notes: A dual-purpose war zone: interference here serves active air-defense / counter-UAS aims, so degradation often tracks kinetic tempo rather than a steady emitter. Expect strong correlation between NIC drops and strike days. Maritime AIS anomalies (ships "on land," "impossible" jumps) are a useful cross-check on aviation ADS-B here.

sources.title: gCaptain — Ukraine Warns Ships Of Scrambled GPS Navigation In Black Sea
sources.note: Ukraine seaports authority July 2024 mariner warning on scrambled/spoofed GNSS.

sources.title: RFE/RL — Suspected Russian GPS Jamming Risks Fresh Dangers In Black Sea Region
sources.note: Regional impact across Romania/Bulgaria/Ukraine; attribution to Russian jamming.

sources.title: Baird Maritime — Ukraine claims offshore platform in Black Sea used by Russia for GPS spoofing
sources.note: Ukrainian claim that a Black Sea gas platform was used for spoofing (struck Aug 2024).

context.typical_causes: Widespread GNSS spoofing degrades navigation integrity for aircraft transiting between Turkey, Cyprus and the Levant, with flight management systems reporting false positions (e.g., near Beirut or Cairo) hundreds of nautical miles from the true track. Cyprus logged thousands of spoofing incidents over a two-month window.
context.known_actors: Reporting and expert analysis largely point to spoofing originating from northern Israel spilling across the sea, rather than from Cyprus itself (per Forbes / OPSGROUP reporting summarized in trade press). See the `levant` region for the more specific source attribution.
context.interpretation_notes: This region is mostly a SPILLOVER footprint of Levant emitters, not an independent source. Read degradation here together with `levant`; rising counts often reflect the same emitter reaching farther, not a new actor. Spoofing (false-but-confident position) is more dangerous to interpret than jamming (signal loss) — flag whether the underlying report is loss-of-signal or position-capture.

sources.title: Forbes (Eric Tegler) — GPS Spoofing in the Middle East Is Now Capturing Avionics
sources.note: Nov 2023 Bombardier Global 7500 case; FMS showed false position near Beirut; eastern Med spoofing.

sources.title: Safe Airspace — Cyprus
sources.note: Operator-facing airspace advisory tracking Cyprus/eastern-Med GNSS spoofing reports.

context.typical_causes: Intense GNSS spoofing over Israel/Lebanon degrades navigation integrity for civil aviation across the region; reporting says spoofing rose sharply after the start of the Gaza war in October 2023 and later spread to central Israel (Tel Aviv/Jerusalem area).
context.known_actors: Widely attributed to Israeli defensive electronic warfare intended to disrupt missile/drone targeting; the IDF acknowledged restricting GPS near the northern border on 15 Oct 2023 (per NPR / Times of Israel). University of Texas researchers (Humphreys/Clements) said they were "highly confident" one major spoofing source was Ein Shemer Airfield near Hadera (per Times of Israel citing NYT).
context.interpretation_notes: Adversarial in mechanism but DEFENSIVE in intent — an important nuance for this archive: the same NIC-degradation signature as Baltic/Black Sea, but the attributed motive is self-protection, not offensive interference. Interpretation should say "attributed to Israeli defensive EW," never assert it flatly. Effects are regional and bleed into `eastern-med`.

sources.title: NPR — Israel's GPS spoofing deters strikes, disrupts planes and apps
sources.note: Attribution to likely IDF spoofing; IDF 15 Oct 2023 acknowledgment of GPS restrictions.

sources.title: Times of Israel — Major GPS disruptions in Middle East coming from IDF airbase (NYT)
sources.note: UT Austin researchers' high-confidence source attribution to Ein Shemer Airfield.

context.typical_causes: GNSS jamming and spoofing degrade navigation integrity for shipping and aircraft in and around the Strait of Hormuz, at times leaving vessels displaying impossible positions (over land, over airports). Interference surged sharply following the June 2025 Israel-Iran conflict and again during escalation in early 2026, affecting well over a thousand vessels per maritime-intelligence firms.
context.known_actors: Attribution is contested. Some reporting says the strait spoofing is "almost certainly" Iran's doing; other researchers attribute much of the surge to non-Iranian systems around the Gulf (Israeli, US and NATO) (per Scientific American / CNN / Windward). Treat attribution here as genuinely unsettled.
context.interpretation_notes: The MOST attribution-ambiguous region in the archive — a crowded electromagnetic environment where multiple state actors plausibly emit. Resist single-actor framing; present the competing attributions side by side. Degradation tracks conflict tempo closely, so date-align interference with `persian-gulf` and `red-sea` events before drawing conclusions.

sources.title: Scientific American — GPS Spoofing Is Scrambling Ships in the Strait of Hormuz
sources.note: Vessels showing impossible positions; discussion of Iran and other possible sources.

sources.title: CNN — Ships and planes are vulnerable to GPS jamming; the Iran war reveals how bad
sources.note: Feb 2026 escalation surge in Hormuz interference; competing attribution.

sources.title: Windward — Widespread GPS Jamming Hits 1,000-plus Ships in the Middle East
sources.note: Maritime-intelligence quantification (1,100+ vessels) of Gulf interference.

context.typical_causes: GNSS jamming and spoofing degrade navigation integrity for shipping in the Red Sea corridor amid the broader Houthi-related shipping crisis; interference has been linked to at least one grounding (the container ship MSC Antonia near Jeddah on 10 May 2025). Levels spiked during the June 2025 Iran-Israel conflict and remained sporadic thereafter.
context.known_actors: Attribution is uncertain. Reporting frames much of the interference as a side effect of military jammers near the conflict zone rather than a single confirmed actor; the MSC Antonia grounding is described as a SUSPECTED spoofing/jamming case, not a proven one (per Maritime Executive / gCaptain / S&P Global). [Actor identity for Red Sea interference — NEEDS SOURCE — author to verify before naming any party.]
context.interpretation_notes: Treat Red Sea interference as conflict spillover with no clean attribution. The MSC Antonia grounding is a valuable *effect* datapoint but its causal link to GNSS interference is inferred, not confirmed — phrase it as suspected. Overlaps heavily with `persian-gulf` in tempo; analyze them together.

sources.title: The Maritime Executive — MSC Containership Aground in Red Sea is a Possible Victim of GPS Jamming
sources.note: MSC Antonia grounding (10 May 2025) framed as possible/suspected GPS-jamming victim.

sources.title: S&P Global — Electronic interference in Red Sea, Hormuz as Houthis renew threats to shipping
sources.note: Red Sea/Hormuz interference context and sporadic persistence through 2025.

context.typical_causes: GNSS jamming degrades navigation integrity across Ukrainian and adjacent NATO-border airspace as a consequence of active air defense and counter-drone electronic warfare during the Russia-Ukraine war; effects extend into neighboring Romanian/Polish airspace.
context.known_actors: Reporting attributes interference in this theater broadly to wartime electronic warfare by both sides, with Russian jamming/spoofing most frequently cited for spillover affecting civil aviation (per Foreign Policy / RFE-RL). [Precise emitter attribution for specific western-Ukraine events — NEEDS SOURCE — author to verify.]
context.interpretation_notes: This is a live combat EW environment; degradation is expected and near-continuous, so ABSENCE of degradation is the more notable signal. Because both belligerents emit, avoid single-actor attribution for the border footprint. Distinguish Ukrainian interior airspace (largely closed to civil traffic) from the NATO-border fringe where most ADS-B observations actually come from.

sources.title: Foreign Policy — War-Zone GPS Spoofing Is Threatening Civil Aviation in the Middle East and Europe
sources.note: Wartime spoofing/jamming spillover into civil aviation around the Ukraine theater.

sources.title: RFE/RL — Suspected Russian GPS Jamming Risks Fresh Dangers In Black Sea Region
sources.note: Cross-border jamming effects into Romania/Bulgaria adjacent to the Ukraine war zone.

context.typical_causes: GNSS jamming degrades navigation integrity for civil aircraft and ships near the inter-Korean maritime border (Northern Limit Line) and around Incheon; South Korea's military reported hundreds of aircraft and ships affected in a late-May/early-June 2024 episode and further incidents in November 2024.
context.known_actors: South Korea's Joint Chiefs of Staff attributed the jamming to North Korea (per Al Jazeera / Korea Times / GPS World). Attribution here rests on the ROK government's public assessment.
context.interpretation_notes: A relatively CONTAINED, episodic pattern compared with the chronic European/Middle-East zones: interference clusters in short bursts near the NLL rather than persisting for months. ADS-B coverage is dense around Incheon, so effects are well observed. Attribution is single-source (ROK JCS) — report it as the ROK assessment, not independently confirmed.

sources.title: Al Jazeera — South Korea's military blames North Korea for GPS signal 'jamming attack'
sources.note: ROK JCS attribution of Nov 2024 jamming affecting civilian aircraft/ships to North Korea.

sources.title: 38 North — North Korean Non-kinetic Attacks: A Problem for Incheon Airport
sources.note: Analysis of North Korean GNSS jamming affecting Incheon; May/Jun 2024 episode figures.

context.typical_causes: BENIGN, SCHEDULED GPS interference test events at US military ranges (e.g., White Sands Missile Range, NM; China Lake, CA; the Nevada Test and Training Range area) degrade navigation integrity in a bounded volume for a bounded time. These are coordinated, published in advance, and de-conflicted with civil aviation.
context.known_actors: The interference source is the US government / military conducting planned tests and training. The FAA publishes these as Notices to Airmen (NOTAMs) / GPS Flight Advisories at least ~24 hours ahead; e.g., the White Sands "WSMRNM GPS" advisory series (per FAA/FAASafety flight advisory PDF and AOPA reporting).
context.interpretation_notes: THIS REGION IS DELIBERATE AND IS THE ARCHIVE'S CONTROL CASE. The raw signal — a drop in Navigation Integrity Category over a defined area — is IDENTICAL to what appears in Kaliningrad or the Black Sea. The difference is entirely in context: here it is announced, geographically fenced, time-boxed, and NOTAM'd. Whenever this archive surfaces a degradation cluster over the US Southwest, the correct first move is to check for a published GPS NOTAM / flight advisory before treating it as anomalous. This region exists to remind the analyst that NIC-degradation alone proves interference, NOT hostility — benign test-range activity and adversarial jamming look the same to the sensor and are separated only by interpretation.

sources.title: FAA / FAASafety — Federal Aviation Administration Flight Advisory: GPS Interference Testing (WSMRNM 26-13)
sources.note: Real FAA flight advisory for scheduled White Sands GPS interference testing (NOTAM'd).

sources.title: AOPA — GPS jamming scheduled across US (military GPS interference exercises)
sources.note: Explains the scheduled, NOTAM-published nature of US military GPS interference exercises.
